perturbation.original_text: A Recipient of Confidential Information under this Agreement shall use the Confidential Information only for the purpose of evaluating a research relationship between the Parties and shall protect such Confidential Information from disclosure to others, using the same degree of care used to protect its own confidentiality or proprietary information of like importance.
perturbation.changed_text: A Recipient of Confidential Information under this Agreement shall use the Confidential Information for the general purpose of exploring a potential relationship between the Parties and shall endeavor to protect such Confidential Information from unwarranted disclosure to unauthorized parties.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase "general purpose of exploring a potential relationship" and "endeavor to protect such Confidential Information from unwarranted disclosure to unauthorized parties" introduces ambiguity as it does not explicitly state the information should only be used for evaluating a research relationship. It also weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. The term 'unwarranted disclosure' is subjective and can be interpreted in several ways, thereby contradicting the strict confidentiality requirements expected under Pennsylvania law regarding trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: PUTSA requires the protection of trade secrets and provides remedies for misappropriation. The modified text weakens the standard of care required, potentially allowing for disclosures that would be considered misappropriation under PUTSA, as the "endeavor to protect" language introduces a lower standard than actively protecting the information to the same degree as one's own confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 12 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5304 (2022) - Damages :: 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 12 - COMMERCE AND TRADE Chapter 53 - Trade Secrets Section 5304 - Damages Universal Citation: 12 PA Cons Stat § 5304 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 5304. Damages. (a) Monetary damages. --Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages can include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator's unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (b) Exemplary damages. --If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection (a). perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the protection of confidential information, creating a contradiction with Pennsylvania law regarding trade secrets. The original text requires protecting Confidential Information "using the same degree of care used to protect its own confidentiality or proprietary information of like importance." In contrast, the changed text uses weaker language: "endeavor to protect such Confidential Information from unwarranted disclosure to unauthorized parties." The term 'unwarranted disclosure' is subjective. 12 PA Cons Stat § 5304 outlines damages for misappropriation, implying a legal expectation to protect trade secrets, not merely endeavor to do so. The change introduces ambiguity, making it harder to prove misappropriation under the PUTSA, as the 'endeavor' standard is much lower than the original's standard.

perturbation.original_text: All such information in tangible form shall be returned to Disclosing Party promptly upon written request by Disclosing Party or the termination or expiration of this Agreement, whichever occurs first, and shall not thereafter be retained in any form by Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: All such information in tangible form should be returned to Disclosing Party upon request, if reasonably feasible, following the termination or expiration of this Agreement, and the Recipient should make an effort not to retain it thereafter.
perturbation.explanation: The change from "shall be returned promptly" to "should be returned upon request, if reasonably feasible" introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to return confidential information. The inclusion of "if reasonably feasible" provides a loophole where the Recipient could argue that returning the information is not feasible, thus contradicting the intended protection of the Disclosing Party's confidential information. Similarly, replacing "shall not thereafter be retained" with "should make an effort not to retain it thereafter" lowers the standard from a strict prohibition to a weaker suggestion.
perturbation.law_explanation: PUTSA grants injunctive relief to prevent the continued misappropriation of trade secrets. The altered language allows for ambiguity in whether or not trade secrets are retained and/or returned. This directly impacts the Disclosing Party's ability to pursue injunctive relief because the contract language weakens the obligation of the Recipient to actively protect the confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 12 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5302 (2022) - Definitions :: 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 12 - COMMERCE AND TRADE Chapter 53 - Trade Secrets Section 5302 - Definitions Universal Citation: 12 PA Cons Stat § 5302 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 5302. Definitions. The following words and phrases when used in this chapter shall have the meanings given to them in this section unless the context clearly indicates otherwise: "Improper means." Includes, but is not limited to, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy or espionage through electronic or other means. "Misappropriation." Includes: (1) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. "Person." A natural person, corporation,
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect trade secrets, potentially contradicting PUTSA's aim to prevent misappropriation through injunctive relief, as indicated in the Law Explanation. The original text's "shall be returned promptly" and "shall not thereafter be retained" are strong obligations. The changed text introduces ambiguity with "should be returned upon request, if reasonably feasible" and "should make an effort not to retain it thereafter." The 'Misappropriation' definition in Scraped Law Snippet 2 includes the 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent,' where the original contract language reduces the risk of such misappropriation by mandating prompt return and no retention. The change introduces a loophole, diminishing the Disclosing Party's ability to prevent misappropriation and thus undermining PUTSA's intent.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall maintain in confidence and shall not disclose to any person not a party hereto, unless permitted to do so under Section 2, or use or exploit in any way, without the Disclosing Party’s written agreement, any Confidential Information for a period of five (5) years from the date of disclosure of such information, unless such information ceases to be Confidential Information prior to the end of such five -year period through no fault of Recipient, or Recipient an d Disclosing Party enter into a written agreement authorizing same.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient should generally aim to maintain in confidence and avoid disclosing to external parties, unless required by some applicable law, any Confidential Information for approximately five (5) years from disclosure, provided the information remains reasonably confidential.
perturbation.explanation: The terms "should generally aim," "avoid disclosing to external parties," "required by some applicable law," and "approximately five years," and "remains reasonably confidential" weaken the obligation to keep information confidential for a defined period. Pennsylvania law requires that trade secrets must be kept confidential. The altered language introduces ambiguity on how long and to what degree the Recipient must keep the information confidential.
perturbation.law_explanation: PUTSA defines a trade secret as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable and is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. By changing the wording to "should generally aim" and "approximately five years," the agreement no longer ensures that reasonable efforts are being made to maintain secrecy, thus conflicting with the definition of a trade secret under PUTSA. Also, the addendum that the information must "remain reasonably confidential" puts the onus on the Disclosing Party to prove there was no breach of confidentiality, which conflicts with the protective intent of PUTSA.

perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality. The original text uses 'shall maintain in confidence,' while the changed text uses 'should generally aim to maintain in confidence,' creating ambiguity. The Law Explanation states that the change to 'should generally aim' and 'approximately five years' no longer ensures reasonable efforts are made to maintain secrecy, conflicting with the requirements for trade secret protection. Although the scraped snippets do not explicitly define trade secrets, the explanation uses PUTSA and implies that the changes undermine the effort to maintain secrecy, which is a crucial element in trade secret law.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees that it will not directly or indirectly transmit, by way of trans - shipment, export, diversion or otherwise, any Confidential Information of the other Party except in accordance with any and all applicable United States expor t control laws and regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party acknowledges their awareness of United States export control laws and regulations and will attempt to avoid transmitting Confidential Information of the other Party in violation of such laws.
perturbation.explanation: The change from "agrees that it will not" to "acknowledges their awareness and will attempt to avoid" weakens the commitment to adhere to export control laws. Pennsylvania courts would uphold that the original text shows that each party would adhere to the U.S. export control laws. Export control laws mandate strict compliance; therefore, any attempt to weaken this obligation would create ambiguities. The term 'attempt to avoid' is vague and does not guarantee compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: The EAR mandates adherence to export control regulations. By weakening the commitment to comply with export control laws, the altered language introduces ambiguity and lowers the standard of compliance, potentially leading to violations of the EAR, as "attempt to avoid" is not equivalent to ensuring compliance.

Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the commitment to adhere to United States export control laws and regulations, creating a contradiction. The original text, "agrees that it will not... transmit... any Confidential Information... except in accordance with... applicable United States expor t control laws and regulations" mandates compliance. The changed text, "acknowledges their awareness... and will attempt to avoid transmitting Confidential Information... in violation of such laws" replaces this mandate with an attempt to avoid violations. This weakens the legal obligation. EAR mandates adherence to export control regulations as stated in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2 highlights the importance of understanding the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. The change from a strict commitment to 'attempt to avoid' introduces ambiguity and a lower standard, potentially leading to non-compliance.
